query: role of IL-18 in thymocyte development and T cell differentiation

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-18 (IL-18)
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophages mostly make IL-18, which can be produced by hepatocytes and keratinocytes. Its principal target is a co-factor in Th1 cell induction. It causes interferon gamma production and enhances NK cell activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-38 (IL-38)
retrieved_docs.content: Il-38 acts on T cells and inhibits the synthesis of IL-17 and IL-22.  The placenta, tonsil's B lymphocytes, spleen, skin, and thymus widely express IL-38. [27]

retrieved_docs.content: Interleukin-2 Interleukin-7 Interleukin-12 Interleukin-18 Interleukin-21

retrieved_docs.title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: B Cells abnormalities: The number of B cells is normal in the majority of patients, but many of them have reduced percentages of isotype-switched memory B cells capable of producing immunoglobin isotypes that are critical toward antibody response recall. The abnormalities that appear to occur in the bone marrow include aberrant gene rearrangement, decreased V gene replacements, reduced diversity of the naïve B cell repertoire, impaired somatic hypermutation, and abnormal expansion of unmutated B cell clones. [13] Toll-like receptor signaling: B cell maturation is defective, and the action of toll-like receptors 7 and 9 (TLR7 and TLR9) becomes impaired because of a yet unknown mechanism. Defective signaling of TLR7 and TLR9 in B cells and plasmacytoid dendritic cells, along with deficient IFN-a, impairs B cell functions and prevents TLR-mediated augmentation of humoral immunity in vivo. [14] [15] The binding of viral RNA to TLR7 or bacterial DNA to TLR9 leads to B cell activation, cytokine secretion, proliferation, and survival; in CVID, the loss of these stimuli functions may contribute to the B cell defects mentioned. [16] [17] Memory B cells: determining the number of isotype-switched memory B cells is of utmost interest because it provides information about the immaturity of the B cell, relates to the numbers of plasma cells in the bone marrow, and gives information about the possible clinical outcomes. [18] Various studies tried to classify CVID patients based on numbers of isotype-switched memory B cells [19] [20] [21] [22] [23] : Patients that switched CD27+IgM-IgD- memory B cells had a higher prevalence of splenomegaly and autoimmune cytopenias. This proposed a fast and reliable new classification for CVID patients with flow cytometric quantification of class-switched memory and immature B cells. Certain disorders were more prevalent in patients with the lowest count of memory B cells (splenomegaly, lymphoid proliferation, granulomatous disease). Lower levels of IgM memory B cells were associated with chronic pulmonary disease (bronchiectasis). Research has determined that the percentages of switched memory B cells changed in some patients over time. Based on these findings, the suggestion is that immunologic characteristics have to be evaluated several times after diagnosis. [24] Decreased T lymphocyte proliferation to mitogens and antigens, [1] reduced T regulatory cells, [25] T cell receptor excision circles that suggest thymic dysregulation, [26] defective T cell receptor signal transduction. Decrease in CD4+ cell count or increase in CD8+ cell count, altering the CD4/CD8 ratio. [27] Increase in CD26 and CD30 levels (the last ones associated with splenomegaly and malignancy) [28] . The minor capacity of dendritic cells to secrete interleukin-12. [29] A drop of plasma cells in the gastrointestinal tract and bone marrow. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Secukinumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Interleukin-17 is a proinflammatory cytokine released by T-helper-17 (Th17) cells. Under the action of interleukin-6 and transforming growth factor-ß, CD4 T cells differentiate into Th17 cells and induce the expression of interleukin-23 receptors (IL-23R) and IL-17. Apart from T-cells, mast cells and neutrophils also secrete IL-17.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoclasts -- Pathophysiology -- T-Cell Mediated Generation of Osteoclasts
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, active T-cells produce pro-inflammatory cytokines, which can stimulate the expression of RANKL on osteoblasts. Inflammatory cytokines that can stimulation osteoclast-mediated bone resorption are IL-1, IL-6, IL-11, IL-15, IL-17, and TNF-alpha. Additionally, IL-1 and TNF-alpha stimulate osteoblasts, which can cause RANKL-induced osteoclasts differentiation by the osteoblasts. Inflammatory cytokines that inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption are IL-4, IL-10, IL-12, IL-13, IL-18, IFN-alpha, and GM-CSF.

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-23 (IL-23)
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophages and dendritic cells mainly synthesize IL-23. It acts on T cells causing maintenance of IL-17 producing T cells. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-15 (IL-15)
retrieved_docs.content: Monocytes, epithelium, and muscles make IL-15. It acts on T cells and activated B cells. It causes the proliferation of both B and T cells. It causes NK cell memory and CD8+ T cell proliferation.

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphoproliferative Disorders -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The new criteria required for the diagnosis of an autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS) [6] include chronic non-malignant lymphoproliferation (lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly over six months of evolution) and elevated peripheral blood double-negative T cells (DNTs). The first accessory in diagnosis is a somatic or a germ-line mutation in ALPS causative gene ( FASL , CASP10 , FAS ) and defective in vitro Fas-mediated apoptosis. Secondary diagnostic criteria are elevated biomarkers (plasma sFASL over 200 pg/ml, plasma or serum vitamin B12 greater than 1500 ng/L, plasma IL-10 greater than 20 pg/ml, and plasma IL-18 greater than 500pg/ml) and immunohistochemical findings on biopsy consistent with ALPS. Other diagnostic signs are polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia, autoimmune cytopenia and a family history of ALPS or non-malignant lymphoproliferation.

retrieved_docs.title: Lofgren Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of lymphocytes within sarcoidal granulomas are CD4+T helper 1 (Th1) lymphocytes, contributing to an overall elevated CD4/CD8 ratio. CD4+T cells secrete interleukin (IL)-2, IL-12, IL-18, and interferon (IFN)-gamma, which in combination with the release of tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-alpha by macrophages and some CD8+T cells, leads to persistent Th1 activity, persistent IFN-gamma elevation and macrophage accumulation within the tissue. Compartmentalization of granuloma-forming CD4+ T lymphocytes and monocytes/macrophages within peripheral tissues leads to systemic lymphopenia and decreased systemic CD4/CD8 ratio.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH17: The early response to bacterial and fungal infections when IL-23 is released instead of IL-12. Releases IL-17, TNF-alpha, and chemokines

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-22 (IL-22)
retrieved_docs.content: Different cells in both innate and acquired immunities produce IL-22, but the primary sources are T cells. Th22 cell is a new line of CD4+ T cells, which differentiated from naive T cells in the presence of various pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6. IL-22 inhibits IL-4 production. It also has essential functions in mucosal surface protection and tissue repair. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Gene knockout mouse studies have provided evidence that the primary IL-2 function in vivo is the suppression of T responses. Mice lacking IL-2 or its receptor (CD25) develop lymphadenopathy and T cell-mediated autoimmunity.

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: IL-40 expression in several human B-cell lymphomas suggests that it may play a role in the pathogenesis of these diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH0: The initial role of activated CD4 T cells, promotes cell immunity by activating dendritic cells and stimulating lymphocyte growth; releases cytokines IL-2, IL-4, and IFN-gamma; can develop into TH1, TH2, TH17, or other TH cells

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-16 (IL-16)
retrieved_docs.content: Eosinophils and CD8+T cells synthesize IL-16. Its principal target is CD4+ T cells. It causes CD4+ T cell chemoattraction.

Similarly, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus” carries the same “النقيلة” relationship, reinforcing the link between thymic metastasis and the general concept of metastatic disease. Clinical associations are highlighted by the relationship between “kanker; metastase” and “سرطان الغدة الدرقية” (thyroid cancer), indicating that metastatic cancer is clinically associated with thyroid malignancy. Moreover, “سرطان الغدة الدرقية” is identified as a child of “ورم صماوي خبيث” (malignant tumor), situating thyroid cancer within the broader class of malignant neoplasms. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how metastatic cancer entities serve as parent concepts for organ‑specific metastatic sites (skin, bone, respiratory/intrathoracic organs, thymus, endocrine glands) and how primary malignant neoplasms—whether well‑defined or of uncertain benign‑malignant status—relate to metastatic potential. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2206
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to respiratory and intrathoracic organ is classified as a specific instance of metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus, indicating that this respiratory and intrathoracic metastatic process falls under the broader category of thymic metastatic malignant neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: The knowledge base describes a network of malignant neoplasms and their metastatic manifestations across multiple organ systems. “kanker; metastase” (cancer metastasis) is positioned as a central concept that gives rise to specific metastatic sites, including “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” (skin metastasis) and “انتشار إلى العظم” (bone metastasis). This same metastatic entity is also linked to “Metastasis to respiratory and intrathoracic organ,” indicating that respiratory and intrathoracic spread is a child of the broader cancer‑metastasis category. In addition, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to endocrine gland” has “kanker; metastase” as a child, and through an inverse‑isa relationship it is recognized that “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus” is a specialized form of endocrine‑gland metastasis, reflecting that thymic involvement is a subset of endocrine‑gland metastatic disease. The hierarchy further connects organ‑specific primary tumors to their metastatic potential. “Maligne

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 159
retrieved_docs.content: and associative relationships create a network in which lymph‑node‑related neoplasms, thymic and other primary malignancies, chest‑origin tumors, thyroid cancer, and metastatic disease across various organ sites are nested within broader malignant‑neoplasm categories, linked to specific anatomic systems, and connected through primary and secondary disease processes. Understanding this network supports accurate classification, diagnosis, and management of hematopoietic, lymphatic, thoracic, and metastatic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: illustrates that the thymus, an endocrine organ, can serve as a metastatic destination. Renal oncocytoma (الورم الأورمي الكلوي) and renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) are specific entities within cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis; partial or complete nephrectomy (استئصال الكلية) is the surgical intervention commonly associated with these renal tumors. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate that malignant cellular infiltrates and malignant or uncertain tumor cells define primary and secondary (metastatic) disease across multiple organ systems—lymph nodes, bone, skin, respiratory and intrathoracic organs, endocrine glands, thymus, breast, ovary, kidney, and pancreas—providing clinicians with a structured framework for cancer origin identification, staging, and targeted therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical classification of neoplasms of the hematopoietic and immune systems places the entity “ورم الغدد الليمفاوية” (lymph‑node tumor) as a narrower concept under “Neoplasm of lymph node” and as a child of “Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified sites”. “سرطان العقد اللمفاوية” (lymph‑node cancer) is a type of “ورم الغدد الليمفاوية”, while “انتشار إلى العقد الليمفاوية” (spread to lymph nodes) is both a parent of “ورم الغدد الليمفاوية” and an inverse manifestation of a lymph‑node neoplasm, linking it to the broader group “الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)” (malignant blood tumors). The anatomic site associated with these entities is the “Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System”. In the metastatic domain, “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of head” is the inverse of “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to head” and both are classified as “secondary malignancies”. Related metastatic concepts include “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular region”, “نقائل إلى الرقبة”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Hematopoietic and Immune Systems is positioned as a narrower concept within the broader category of ورم الغدد الليمفاوية (lymph‑node tumor). Within this hierarchy it is linked to both Hodgkin’s disease and the entity labeled cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the classification encompasses lymph‑node malignancies that are not otherwise specified and includes Hodgkin‑type disease. Cancer; other and unspecified primary itself is placed under ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, is mapped to thymický karcinom (thymic carcinoma), and contains the member group الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ) (malignant blood‑cell tumors), reflecting its role as a catch‑all category for diverse hematologic and lymphoid malignancies. Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified sites is described as having a broader relationship to ورم الغدد الليمفاوية and is also connected to انتشار إلى العقد الليمفاوية (spread to lymph nodes), indicating that metastatic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE HEMATOPOIETIC AND IMMUNE SYSTEMS is situated within a hierarchical cancer framework, with Hodgkin’s disease and the broader category “cancer; other and unspecified primary” as higher‑level parents. “Cancer; other and unspecified primary” functions as an umbrella term that encompasses the malignant blood tumor group الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ), is inversely related to ورم خبيث في الصدر (malignant tumor in the chest), and corresponds to the Czech term thymický karcinom (thymic carcinoma). Within this hierarchy, ورم الغدد الليمفاوية (lymph‑node tumor) is a parent of both “cancer; other and unspecified primary” and Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified sites, and it subsumes specific entities such as سرطان العقد اللمفاوية (lymph‑node cancer) and Neoplasm of lymph node. Neoplasm of lymph node is linked inversely to انتشار إلى العقد الليمفاوية (spread to lymph nodes), which in turn is inversely related to سرطان العقد اللمفاوية and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Disease Terminology is a classification framework that encompasses a range of disease concepts, including التهاب الغِشاء المُخاطِيّ للمعدة والأمعاء, which refers to inflammation of the mucosal lining of the stomach and intestines, and thymický karcinom, a malignant tumor originating in the thymus gland. By incorporating both gastrointestinal mucosal inflammation and thymic carcinoma, CTRP Disease Terminology provides a structured reference for these distinct yet clinically relevant conditions.

Overall, the knowledge base integrates metastatic and secondary lesion concepts with primary cancer sites, clarifies the distinction between benign and malignant tumor cells, maps hierarchical relationships among organ‑specific metastases (especially thymic and endocrine gland involvement), and connects procedural interventions such as nephrectomy to their associated renal malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 934
retrieved_docs.content: The classification of metastatic malignant neoplasms includes several organ‑specific categories. The entity “Metastasis to respiratory and intrathoracic organ” is linked by a “has child” relationship to the generic concept “kanker; metastase” and, according to another triple, has “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus” as its parent. The “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to endocrine gland” also “has child” the same generic “kanker; metastase” and is connected to “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus” through an “inverse_isa” relationship, indicating that thymic metastasis is a more specific subclass of endocrine‑gland metastasis. Additionally, the generic metastasis term “kanker; metastase” is recorded as a child of the Arabic‑named entity “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” (metastatic skin cancer). The relationship terms “has child”, “has parent”, and “inverse_isa” define the hierarchical structure of these disease entities. Together, these connections illustrate how metastatic cancers are organized by primary organ of spread, with broader categories encompassing more specific sites such as the thymus, and how a generic metastasis concept relates to both organ‑specific and skin‑specific metastatic disease, aiding clinicians in classification, diagnosis, and management.
